speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife Miriam Jung. How long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 45 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you talked about your experiences with each other, with your children, over those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the children, we did talk about it the first time, I think, when they did learn in school about it. When they did learn about World War II, they came home, and they asked. And then, I don't know how long [? it was, ?] a movie Holocaust was out. It was a series a few nights. And we let them watch it. And this was when we talked with them about it again. We did never talk too much about it at home. But now last year, because of the 50th anniversary, there was lots of talk about it. And we did also take them to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you revisit your hometowns?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Czechoslovakia for 12 days. We went first to Prague, and then next day was in Terezin, Theresienstadt. And then we took them to the place of birth from [INAUDIBLE], and where I was born, where I did live. And then we had a few days fun in [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You are also a survivor of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's why we went to Terezin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message, now that it's 50 years after the war-- a message for your children and grandchildren and future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want tell this. Humanity should learn from mistake what's happened and should appreciate the United States and life in United States. Only, we still have lots of-- lots of injustice in the world. And I hope so, they will stop it and learn from it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It is very sad. These people can live together and-- and without hatred, no matter what they believe or what they think they-- they should be learned in school and-- and let everybody live in peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Jung? Do you have a message?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say the same. But unfortunately, I think that people will never learn to live in peace, no matter what.

speaker: interviewer
text: After everything that you've been through, after all of your experiences, could you describe what your faith is today-- your religious faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are agnostics.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are agnostics, so am I

speaker: interviewee
text: We believe in science. Well, I say this, more people they were killed in name of God than saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we raise our children with the 10 Commandments, I think, without the religious background. Just because those are really the laws by which people should live no matter what they do or don't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jung, who is this photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the photograph of my parents. My mother, Jolana Jung, born Klein, and-- and this is my father Alexander Jung. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The picture was taken in Presov. This is their wedding picture that took place in 1925. And there was a big wedding [LAUGHS] that took day and night. There was a big celebration. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a me in Presov. I was four years old and showing off my nice birthday present, what I received from our family. This was a big deal. I-- I was the only one in the whole street having such a toy. And we had lots of fun with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ladislav Jung. And I was born also in Presov.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where this photo is taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is where this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my Grandmother Klein, born Hermina Eva Friedman. And next to her is her daughter. This is my Aunt Alizabeta. She was one of my favorite aunt, who give me refuge after the war when I return from concentration camp. And below is me in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was four years old-- five years old. And like you see, all the dressing that I wear-- and what was all custom made. There was nothing bought from the-- from the stores. We had everything-- all the luxury and everything what I wear was handmade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're Ladislav Jung here. This was taken in Presov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this was taken in Presov. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1935. This is my father, Alexander Jung, born in 1900. And next to him is me, Ladislav Jung. We-- we did hikes-- lots of hikes, outdoors camping with my father. There were lots of mineral springs and beautiful woods. And on weekends, he would spend with me and do lots of hiking and different sports, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this near Presov?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is near Presov taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do we see in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in this photograph, you see father of my mother, my Grandfather Klein. He was born in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name, full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Izak Klein, and her daughter Alizabeta Klein. And this is in front of the house, of the bakery, and also the house where I spent a lot of time of my life. There was a house what never stopped, that was going all the time, 24 hours a day. He-- he was a baker. And on the wall there was the advertising. I, what means Izak. And Klein, this is the last name. And 'pekar' means bakery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Presov.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occasion of this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was picnicking with friends from my mother and my aunt by a river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is near Presov.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must be somewhere around 1932. And they get together with friends. And on the-- on the-- on the picture, on the second from the right is my mother. And then third one from the left is my Aunt Alizabeta, what I-- what I mentioned before, in Presov. And then below is mine uncle, brother from Alizabeta, and my mother's brother, my uncle Alexander Klein. And he holding me in the arms there, like a little boy. I am still there-- photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Ladislav Jung, in-- of age, about 6. And this was taken-- I start to learn how to ride a bike. And this was on the side backyard of the house taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Presov?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Presov. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is photo in-- taken in picture in Presov. This is-- on the left is my grandfather, the father of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Izak Klein. And next to her is her daughter and sister of my mother, Alizabeta Klein taken in Presov. And the pictures were given to me, some of them, by aunt and some by friends. And they were hidden. And they survived during the-- the war. So I have very few pictures [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what were left what I found.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the survivors of your family are this aunt, Alizabeta.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The aunt and the uncle in-- in United States, who was here. And this-- and my cousin, they are the closest survivor of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in army while I working in repairing the personnel carrier in Israel in-- in time 1948, '49. And this is American-made personnel carrier. They were very good, saved lots of lives. Well, they were armored. And also, they had defense. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had machine gun in front. So they were-- saved lots of lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- And this is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me working and taking care of maintaining the vehicles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ladislav Jung.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ladislav Jung. [LAUGHS] This is me in the middle, Ladislav Jung, sitting in the yard and resting with our friends. We maintained the armored vehicle. It is an English Panzer-- a English Panzer vehicle with 2-pounder cannon on it, machine guns-- two machine guns, one mounted on top and one by the driver in the tower. And the engines then there were the four flat head engines. And they was very hard maintenance while everything was heavy. And [? like you ?] [? in ?] desert with burning temperature between 100 and 120 degrees, sometimes you couldn't hold the range. Or, if you rode inside, in bottle, later on we were half-naked. [LAUGHS] We had just coverall on. And we were bathing in own perspirement. We didn't have air conditioners. This is in Presov in 1995 when we return to-- it was the 50th anniversary in Terezin. So we visited Presov. On mine right is Miriam Jung second from the left. And I am to her right, Ladislav Jung. Then all the way to the left is my-- is-- my youngest son, Ted Alexander Jung. And then next to me is Daniel Jung, is the number two son. And on extreme right is Rafael Jung, the oldest son.

speaker: interviewer
text: This document was executed in 1904. And it is proof of citizenship in the Austro-Hungarian Empire in an area which would later become Czechoslovakia. And which of your ancestors is named in this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this document is my great-grandfather. And this is the proof of the citizenship. It's handwritten. And you see the stamp is one krone, what was then the currency of the Austrian Empire. And you had to put a stamp to make it legal. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your great-grandfather's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Shamu-- in Hungarian-- Shamu Jung, born in 1857 in [INAUDIBLE]. And this document is handwritten in the Hungarian language. Or, this was the language-- the official language of the-- of the-- of this community. Actually, part of the empire was Hungarian, Austrian, and part of Romania, and part of--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family was in this area since at least this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
